result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before the pen touches, when the whole blank acre is still possible. Not the story, not even the sentence—just the hush in the nerve ends. A thousand words is a room with one door you haven’t opened yet; you stand in the hallway of your own skull, listening for furniture moving inside. I used to believe I had to bring a flashlight. Now I think the dark itself is the gift.  

There is a city built entirely of almost. It hums a half-tone below the music of arrival. Its citizens walk around with grocery bags full of ingredients for dinners they will never cook, wearing coats they keep forgetting to button. They wave at each other across plazas constructed from good intentions mortared with regret. No one is ever late; clocks are only mirrors. I lived there for thirty-three years without noticing the address. When I finally left, the bridge behind me dissolved into a ribbon of typewriter tape, fluttering like a cat that has decided against coming indoors.

My father collected unopened boxes of staples. He said they were tiny steel constellations waiting to bind something together, maybe the sky. After he died we found them stacked in the attic like edible silver bricks. I took one, hid it in my desk, and sometimes at 2 a.m. when the paragraphs rebel, I shake it like a maraca. The sound is small and certain: a regiment of possible attachments marching in place. I have never used a single staple. The possibility keeps the pages looser, more honest; they rustle like birds arguing about which way is south.

Time is not a river; it is a drawer full of knives you have already held. You open it looking for a letter, instead your thumb remembers the serrated edge of a camping trip, the orange handle of the knife you used to whittle a whistle that never whistled. Blood is just punctuation. The scar is the sentence that ended there, but the story keeps climbing uphill toward another punctuation mark we pretend is final. I keep trying to write without verbs, because motion is a lie we agreed to tell ourselves so the earth feels rented rather than stolen.

Yesterday a woman on the train asked if I believed in angels. She had cilantro in her hair and a tattoo of a city skyline wrapping her throat like a purple necklace. I told her I believe in unfinished apology letters that arrive the day after the recipient moves away. She nodded as if I had shown her the exact bus route to her next destination, then offered me a tangerine so small it could have been a planet orbiting an unseen child. The segments came apart like secrets told in a war. We ate in silence while the carriage sliced through suburbs neither of us could afford to die in.

The trouble with mirrors is they remember every face you forgot to make. I keep one turned to the wall like a punished child. At night it hums, attempting to practice the expressions I denied it. Sometimes I flip it around and watch the accumulation rush toward me—every version of myself that believed this was the final draft. They wave, a crowd of redundant prototypes, each holding a blue pencil behind his back. I wave back, erase them with breath, start again. The glass is warm, as if all those selves have been running a fever for decades.

I want to write a sentence so long it needs to sit down halfway through, a sentence that borrows your shoe to rest its blistered metaphor. It would begin in a ticket queue in Lisbon and end inside the echo of a kettle boiling in a house you haven’t bought yet. Along the way it would collect postcards, grocery lists, the exact smell of burnt sugar at the fair where you first understood that love is a Ferris wheel that keeps stopping just long before the top. The sentence would apologize for taking so much of your life, then hand it back laundered and inside-out, pockets full of sand from a beach you forgot you visited.

Children know that if you step on a crack you fracture something more serious than a mother’s spine; you splinter the narrative. Watch them hopscotch: each landing is a revision, a refusal to endorse the linear. They are drafting a map of prohibitions that later they will call imagination. We adults paved the map, renamed it sidewalk, then wondered why the journey feels hard beneath our shoes. I try to remember the last time I traveled by jumping. Instead I calculate the number of cracks between here and the post office, translate them into overdue bills, feel the ache propagate upward like a vine that learned to type.

The ocean is a library that never acquired the concept of silence. Every shell is an overdue book screaming to be returned to the wave-librarian who will press it wetly against your ear and whisper the exact temperature at which your most important yes dissolved. People collect shells as if ownership could muffle the plot. I keep one in my mouth during difficult conversations; it tastes of shipwreck and postage due. When I finally speak, sentences arrive wearing life vests, reluctant to disembark. They know the statistics: most words drown within sight of the shore they were trying to describe.

There should be a museum of almost-ink. One room would display grocery receipts bearing the first line of a poem aborted by the sudden collapse of a checkout queue. Another would loop security footage of waiters pausing, tray in hand, while the sentence they will never write finishes itself inside their frontal lobe. Visitors would receive a blank napkin at the turnstile; at the exit, an unseen scanner would reveal what they subconsciously scribbled. No one would be allowed to keep the napkin; it would be archived, burned, launched into the stratosphere where the ashes learn to rain back down as marginalia.

Tonight the moon looks like a coin somebody flicked to decide whether the story merited continuation. It spins on the black felt of sky, neither heads nor tails, just the glint of possibility wobbling toward equilibrium. I stand beneath it barefoot, having misplaced my shoes somewhere around paragraph three. The pavement is cold, then warm, as if the earth itself alternates between holding on and letting go. Somewhere a staple clicks its tiny heels, a drawer of knives rehearses applause, a sentence too long pauses to tie its imaginary lace. I open my hand. The tangerine planet rolls out, orbiting nothing, and for a moment the whole blank acre is still possible.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
